title: Havea Apparel
text:
Overview
Havea is a direct-to-consumer outdoor-performance activewear brand I co-founded with my girlfriend, built for hikers, rock climbers, runners, and anyone who wants gear that doesn’t force a trade-off between comfort, performance, and affordability. The line — sports bras, leggings, and jackets — is built around fabrics we test ourselves for breathability, stretch, and recovery, with a brand promise we call “The Joy of Doing.”
Where my co-founder leads product design and brand direction, my focus has been the commercial and data engine behind the brand: e-commerce analytics, paid acquisition, and the reporting that tells us which products, channels, and campaigns are actually working.




My Role & Contributions

Designed and implemented end-to-end marketing analytics for the Shopify storefront, including conversion tracking, funnel dashboards, and multi-touch campaign attribution
Set up and manage Meta Ads Manager campaigns targeting the outdoor/athletic customer segment
Built recurring performance reporting connecting ad spend, site traffic, and conversion data into a single view
Manage customer data and retention workflows through CRM tooling




Brand & Product

Product lines: sports bras, leggings, jackets — built for hiking, rock climbing, running, and highlining
Differentiator: in-house fabric testing for stretch, recovery, breathability, and fit across body types
Positioning: performance-grade gear at a price point that doesn’t require choosing between quality and affordability





The Analytics Side
A DTC apparel brand lives or dies on knowing which creative, channel, and product combination actually converts — and Shopify’s native reporting only tells part of that story. I built out conversion and funnel tracking that connects ad-platform data (Meta) with on-site behavior and purchase data, so we can see attribution across the full path, not just last-click. That’s let us make faster, more confident decisions about where to put ad spend and which products to feature first.


Learning Outcomes

Standing up an e-commerce analytics stack from scratch on a real, revenue-generating Shopify store
Running paid social campaigns end-to-end: targeting, creative testing, budget allocation, and attribution
What co-founding a physical-product brand with a partner actually requires — from vendor sourcing to first sale

Visit Havea Apparel →

title: BreakPlate
text:
Overview
BreakPlate (a product of TIMEFRAME, LLC) is the venture I’m currently building with my friend and co-founder, McGwire Herbert. It’s a real-time wage-and-hour compliance platform built specifically around California labor law — the same law that has produced billion-dollar-scale liability for employers who couldn’t prove a break was actually offered.
The core insight: payroll platforms like Rippling, Gusto, and ADP protect employers from the IRS — but none of them protect against a PAGA (Private Attorneys General Act) class-action attorney, because a timesheet isn’t legal proof that a break happened. BreakPlate closes that gap by generating a biometrically-authenticated, law-referenced audit trail at every clock-out, automatically.




How It Works

Real-time enforcement: compliance checks run at clock-out, not days later at payroll sync — so violations can still be remediated the same shift
Biometric attestation: break offers are confirmed via Face ID / Touch ID, tying the record to the employee’s identity so it can’t be falsified after the fact
AI law ingestion: compliance rules update automatically as California (and soon Nevada, Arizona, and other states) amend labor code — no manual patching




The Market Problem

California wage-and-hour liability exceeds $2B annually
TJ Maxx, Starbucks, and AlliedBarton alone have settled $42.5M+ combined for missed-break violations
Employers who take documented “reasonable steps” see roughly an 85% reduction in PAGA penalties
Zero existing payroll platforms can actually prove a break was offered — that’s the gap BreakPlate fills





My Role & Contributions
As co-founder, I lead go-to-market strategy and the marketing/growth analytics side of the business — positioning BreakPlate against incumbent payroll and HR platforms, building the pricing and outbound strategy for California employers, and setting up the data infrastructure to track pipeline, trial conversion, and customer acquisition cost as we move toward launch and fundraising. I’m also applying the same AI-agent and automation experience from my consulting work to help shape how BreakPlate’s compliance-monitoring product is positioned to both HR buyers and legal/risk stakeholders.


Learning Outcomes

Building GTM strategy for a category-creating B2B SaaS product (compliance-as-defense, not just compliance-as-checklist)
Translating a technical/legal product (AI law ingestion, biometric attestation) into a story that lands with HR and risk-management buyers
Structuring an early-stage startup for investor conversations — market sizing, liability data, and a defensible wedge against much larger payroll incumbents

Visit BreakPlate →

title: Building Data-Driven Companies
text:
Who I Am

Co-founder, operator, and marketing-analytics practitioner
8+ years turning messy multi-channel data into decisions
Currently building across three companies: event medicine, DTC apparel, and compliance SaaS
Former NCAA Decathlete — comfortable being good at many things at once

title: Building Data-Driven Companies
text:
The Problem I Keep Solving

Founders and executives don’t lack data. They lack a trustworthy pipeline from raw data to a decision.

Across every company I’ve worked on, the pattern is the same:

Data lives in 4–6 disconnected systems (CRM, ad platforms, POS, ops tools)
No one owns reconciling it
Decisions get made on vibes, not verified numbers

My job has consistently been building the layer that fixes that.

title: Building Data-Driven Companies
text:
Case 1 — Prevent Medical Solutions
Problem: Scaling from a startup to 580+ employees meant staffing and budget decisions had to be made before an event happened, not reconciled after.
What I built:

Forecast models linking contracted event volume → staffing needs → labor cost
Board-level KPI dashboards tracking valuation-relevant metrics
Workforce-analytics pipeline supporting multi-site hiring at speed

title: Building Data-Driven Companies
text:
Case 2 — Havea Apparel
Problem: A DTC Shopify brand needs to know which channel × creative × product combination actually converts — not just last-click attribution.
What I built:
# Simplified shape of the attribution join
# (ad platform export + Shopify orders + on-site sessions)

attributed = (
    sessions
    .merge(ad_spend, on=["utm_campaign", "date"])
    .merge(orders, on="session_id", how="left")
    .assign(cac = lambda d: d.spend / d.orders.replace(0, pd.NA))
)
Meta Ads Manager + GA4 + Shopify orders, joined into one attribution table — so ad spend decisions are made on margin, not just click volume.

title: Building Data-Driven Companies
text:
Case 3 — BreakPlate
Problem: California wage-and-hour liability is a $2B+/year problem, and no payroll platform can prove a break was offered.
The product’s technical core:

Real-time compliance checks at clock-out (not batch, days later)
Biometric attestation (Face ID / Touch ID) tied to each record
AI-driven ingestion of statutory changes across jurisdictions

My role: GTM strategy, positioning against payroll incumbents, and the growth-analytics stack (pipeline, trial conversion, CAC) as we move toward launch.

title: Projects
text: I’ve spent the last several years building companies rather than just studying them — which means every project below comes with real revenue, real employees, and real compliance headaches attached. Below are the three ventures I’ve co-founded, spanning healthcare event services, consumer apparel, and B2B compliance SaaS. Each write-up covers the business problem, my role, the data/analytics systems I built, and the results.

title: Projects
text:
What You’ll Find

Scaling operations with data: how I used financial models and workforce analytics to help grow Prevent Medical Solutions to 580+ employees.
Brand & e-commerce analytics: building the marketing and customer data foundation for a DTC apparel brand.
Product & GTM strategy for an early-stage SaaS startup: how BreakPlate is using data to sell compliance software into a $2B+ liability market.

title: Prevent Medical Solutions
text:
Overview
Prevent Medical Solutions is an event-medical and public-safety services company I co-founded in 2022. We provide scalable, ICU-level medical activations for concerts, festivals, marathons, sporting events, and corporate gatherings — everything from a 200-person community race to events with 185,000+ attendees. Between 2023 and 2025 alone, the company has protected more than 6.1 million attendees and staff across Southern California and beyond.
I hold the Co-Founder / President / Board Member role, and my day-to-day has centered on the financial and data infrastructure that let a company scale from a startup idea to 580+ employees and a $15M valuation in under four years.




My Role & Contributions

Designed and maintained the financial planning models, budget forecasts, and KPI dashboards the board and executive team use to make strategic calls
Built workforce-analytics and hiring-pipeline reporting to support rapid, multi-site headcount scaling
Negotiated and managed vendor/event-organizer contracts, using performance and ROI data to drive terms
Directed operational data strategy across dispatch, staffing, and multi-site event logistics




Business Impact

Scaled from founding team to 580+ employees
Reached a $15M company valuation
Protected 6.1M+ attendees & staff (2023–2025)
Capable of activating 80+ bed medical centers and full ICU-level care at events up to 185,000 attendees
Deployed FirstNet/CAD-integrated real-time tracking (OnTrack) across event sites





The Data & Systems Side
Running a company that scales its headcount up and down with the events calendar means the ordinary HR and finance playbook doesn’t quite work. I built recurring reporting cycles that tie staffing forecasts to contracted event volume, so budget owners can see labor costs and margin per event type well before the event happens — rather than reconciling after the fact. That same discipline carries into vendor and vendor-performance tracking, where we use structured data (not just spreadsheets passed around) to decide who gets rebooked.


Learning Outcomes

What it actually takes to build financial and workforce reporting systems that scale with headcount, not against it
How to translate multi-stakeholder (board, ops, field staff) needs into a single reporting framework
Negotiating from a position backed by real performance data, not gut feel

Visit Prevent Medical Solutions →

title: Thomas Prata
text:
About Me
I am working towards a B.S. in Business Administration (Marketing) from California State Polytechnic University, Pomona, where I also competed as an NCAA Track & Field athlete as a Decathalete. That’s more or less how I’ve approached my career since: rather than picking one lane, I’ve built the range to run finance, operations, marketing, and analytics at the same time, which is exactly what early stage companies need from a co-founder.
Since 2022 I’ve been building Prevent Medical Solutions from the ground up, alongside launching Havea, an apparel brand I co-founded with my girlfriend, and most recently BreakPlate, a labor-compliance automation platform I’m building with my friend and co-founder McGwire Herbert. Details on all three are on the Projects page.

Highlighted Achievements

Co-founded and scaled Prevent Medical Solutions from a startup to 580+ employees
Designed the financial models, KPI dashboards, and workforce-analytics systems the board uses to make strategic decisions
Currently run marketing analytics engagements for 6+ retainer clients, spanning law firms, professional services, and consumer brands
Deployed AI-powered client-acquisition agents that measurably increased lead volume for legal-industry clients
